input.user_input: Explain the attorney-client privilege protections available to a corporate client when its employees communicate with in-house legal counsel.

actual:
The attorney-client privilege protects confidential communications between a client and their attorney made for the purpose of obtaining or providing legal advice. When the client is a corporation, the privilege applies to communications with both outside counsel and in-house counsel. However, applying the privilege in the corporate context—particularly with in-house counsel—introduces unique complexities.

Here is an overview of how the attorney-client privilege protects corporate clients when their employees communicate with in-house counsel.
